narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, including choosing full-time studies ahead. Our admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated your chance of failing the bar at 45.8%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger projected outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, slightly under the 3.21 average, actually worked in your favor, suggesting underlying potential that countered some concerns. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 5, close to the average of 5.71, and your full-time enrollment—shared by over 90% of students—both nudged the prediction toward bar passage success. Your LSAT score of 29, however, lower than the usual 36.7, contributed somewhat toward the failure risk, while family income had a minor influence in that direction. Overall, your profile stands out as having inconsistent academic deciles relative to typical admits, whose stronger Year 3 showings often tip the scales. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive, but a data-driven call to protect the school's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or tutoring, and reapplying with an updated profile that aligns closer to top performers—many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps. We're rooting for you to turn this around and join the ranks of successful lawyers. Keep pushing ahead.
